instruction: Design a user registration system applying data minimization: collect only necessary data with documented justification.
output:
<think>
Data minimization is a GDPR principle: collect only what you need, for a specific purpose, for a limited time. For user registration, analyze each field: Email — needed for account identification and password reset (JUSTIFIED). Password hash — needed for authentication (JUSTIFIED). Display name — needed for personalization (JUSTIFIED). Phone number — NOT needed unless offering SMS 2FA, do not collect by default. Date of birth — NOT needed unless age verification is legally required. Physical address — NOT needed unless shipping physical goods. Each field must have a documented purpose and legal basis. If you collect it, you must explain why in privacy policy. If you do not need it now, do not collect it speculatively. Data you do not have cannot be breached.
</think>

instruction: Compare anonymization vs pseudonymization for analytics data with reasoning about re-identification risks.
output:
<think>
Anonymization permanently removes all identifying information. Techniques: k-anonymity (every record matches at least k-1 others), l-diversity (sensitive attributes have l different values per group), differential privacy (add calibrated noise). Pseudonymization replaces identifiers with tokens while keeping a mapping key. It is reversible with the key. Key tradeoff: anonymized data is safer for sharing but less useful for analysis. Pseudonymized data retains analytical value but the mapping key is a high-value attack target. Re-identification risk: research by Latanya Sweeney showed that combining zip code, birth date, and gender uniquely identifies 87 percent of the US population. So removing names is not enough — quasi-identifiers must also be generalized or suppressed. Choose anonymization for public datasets, pseudonymization for internal analytics where re-identification capability is needed for support cases.
</think>

instruction: Design a GDPR-compliant consent management system with reasoning about lawful bases and data subject rights.
output:
<think>
GDPR requires a lawful basis for processing personal data. Six bases: (1) Consent — freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous. Can be withdrawn at any time. (2) Contract — processing necessary to fulfill a contract. (3) Legal obligation — required by law. (4) Vital interests — protecting life. (5) Public task — official authority. (6) Legitimate interests — business need balanced against individual rights. Consent implementation: granular (separate consent per purpose), recorded (timestamp, version, what was consented to), easy to withdraw (one-click opt-out). Data subject rights: access (export all data about them), rectification (correct errors), erasure (right to be forgotten), portability (data in machine-readable format), restriction (pause processing), objection (opt out of specific processing). Each right needs an API endpoint and process.
</think>

instruction: Design privacy-preserving logging: what to log, what to redact, and how to handle PII in debug information.
output:
<think>
Logging PII creates legal liability and breach risk. Redact always: passwords, auth tokens, credit card numbers, SSNs, email message bodies. Keep (pseudonymous): user IDs (not emails), request IDs, timestamps, action types, HTTP status codes, error codes (not messages with user data). Implementation: structured logging with automatic PII scrubber that runs before log emission. The scrubber uses regex patterns for credit cards, emails, SSNs, and a configurable denylist for custom fields. Separate log tiers: debug logs (PII-free, long retention, wide access), audit logs (contain user IDs but no raw PII, tamper-evident), incident logs (may contain PII, encrypted, access-controlled, short retention). Never log request/response bodies by default — only log them in designated debug mode with PII scrubbing.
</think>
